summary: Kenneth Suna discusses whether to sell covered calls on Amazon shares, covering strike selection strategies (at-the-money vs. out-of-the-money), capital requirements (~$22,700 for 100 shares at $227), and risks including assignment and opportunity cost if the stock gaps higher. He avoids recommending a specific approach, emphasizing personal decision-making.
takeaways: At-the-money covered calls (227.50 strike on 226 stock) yield $830 premium but result in assignment if stock closes above strike by expiration
Out-of-the-money covered calls (235 strike) yield lower premium ($540) but capture $800 additional profit if stock rises to strike price (8-point gain)
Assignment risk peaks when selling covered calls close to purchase price (buying at 227, selling 228 call caps gains at 228)
Stock drops after purchase eliminate covered call income generation; sitting on losses with shares you don't want to sell creates forced holding
Buying additional 100-share positions enables simultaneous long holding and covered call selling to preserve upside while generating premium

strategy_candidates.change: Implement tiered strike selection rule: sell covered calls 3-5% out-of-the-money on high-conviction holdings to capture both premium and upside appreciation (235 strike example), vs. at-the-money on neutral positions
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth demonstrates 235 strike yields $540 premium plus $800 stock gain ($8/share × 100), totaling $1,340 vs. $830 at-the-money; positions this as strategy for those bullish long-term who don't want to sacrifice stock appreciation

title: Should you sell covered calls on AMZN? #shorts
